The eight K under rBook though — that's real. Here's why: retail comps are asking forty grand, but they're not all moving. This market's slower than it was. We're priced to move it fast, which for you means floor plan relief and cash flow.

On the odometer variance — I'm not hiding anything. Carfax came back at fifty-five K, auction showed fifty-nine eight. It's disclosed, title's clean, no other damage flags. Both Carfax and AutoCheck are blank otherwise. It's not ideal, but it's transparent, and the price reflects it.

What I'm not gonna do is tell you there's something mechanically wrong when there isn't. This car's solid — the variance is the disclosure, and you've

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Alright Bill, let me get this straight. You're saying MMR is $27k, but your wholesale price is $31,500, which puts it closer to retail without being upside down if you need to move it quickly. 

The $8k difference from rBook's price is because the retail comps are higher but not all of them are selling. So you're pricing it to sell faster and give me cash flow and floor plan relief.

And while Carfax shows 55k miles, auction reports 59,800. You say it's just a variance, both reports are clean otherwise, title is clean with no other damage flags. It's solid but the variance is all you're hiding.

I get that retail buyers want everything perfect and mechanics will kill deals over millage. But on my end, I've been burned by folks stretching or outright lying about what they knew. So I need it straight-up from you.

What's your track record? What do other buyers say about your inventory accuracy and condition reporting? Because I'm not just buying a car here - I'm taking a leap based on trust in you to deliver exactly as advertised.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Mike, I respect that. You've been burned, so you need real answers, not sales talk.

Here's what I can tell you straight: I'm one guy making calls. My job is to know the inventory cold and not waste your time with garbage. If I pitch you something and it shows up wrong, that kills the deal AND the relationship. I'm not incentivized to lie to you — I make money on volume and repeat business, not on one unit.

On this seven-fifty specifically — everything in that bid card is what we've got. Carfax report, AutoCheck report, the variance flagged. No surprises when it rolls up.

Beyond that, honestly, I don't have a formal track record sheet in front of me to pull — that's something you'd want to ask Oscar or one of the owners about directly. They can walk you through dealer feedback and repeat buy patterns. What I can tell you is we wouldn't be in business if we were moving junk or misreporting condition.

Here's what I'm thinking: let me send you the full bid card right now — Carfax, photos, all the option list, the damage audit,

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
That makes sense, Bill. It sounds like you're focused on quality over quantity, which is good to hear. A clean track record with other dealers would certainly help build my trust in you.

Sending over that full bid card with everything disclosed upfront could be a great way to show me what I'm getting into without any surprises. The photos and option list especially will give me a clear picture of what condition the car is in.

I appreciate you taking the time to handle my concerns directly instead of just trying to make the sale. That transparency matters.

Tell you what - let's get that bid card over to me. I'll take some time to review everything, check out the Carfax and AutoCheck reports, look at those photos and condition notes. If it looks good to me after digging in, maybe we can discuss moving forward on this BMW seven-fifty-i.

But like I said earlier - I'm not going to jump into anything without doing my due diligence first. So give me that full bid card package, let me dig in, and then we'll see what happens from there.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Done. You've got it. Everything's there — Carfax, AutoCheck, the full option breakdown, the variance flagged. Take your time with it.

When you're ready, shoot me back. If you've got questions after you dig in — condition details, any of the options, whatever — I'm here. And if you want to move on it, we'll get one of the owners to lock it down with you properly.

Good talking to you, Mike.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Sounds good, Bill. I appreciate the follow-through and willingness to answer my additional questions.

I'll take a thorough look at everything and reach back out when I'm ready. It's nice to have this kind of transparency and customer service for once.

Talk to you soon, and thanks again."

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Talk to you soon, Mike. Thanks for the time.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: *chuckles* Yeah, it's a breath of fresh air in my experience. Look forward to catching up with the rest."

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Catch you later, Mike.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Hi Mike, this is Bill from Experience Wholesale. Do you have a moment?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: Oh, hey Bill. Yeah, I suppose I've got a minute or two. What's on your mind?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Great. So we just caught a twenty-twenty-one BMW seven-fifty-i. Fifty-nine thousand eight hundred miles, black sapphire, loaded with the Executive Package and Driving Assistance Pro. Here's where I'm at — thirty-one five.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: Hmm, sounds fancy. But how do you know it's clean? There's gotta be a story behind that low mileage for a car that old.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Yeah, good catch. So heads up — there's an odometer mismatch. The bid came in at fifty-nine eight, but Carfax is showing fifty-five two-eight-three. We flagged that on intake, so it's on the table. That said, both Carfax and AutoCheck are clean otherwise — no damage, no accidents, no titles.

The car's mechanically solid and loaded out. You're looking at almost one hundred and eleven grand in original MSRP. And here's the real thing — rBook comps on this thing are trading up around forty grand, so at thirty-one five, you're nearly nine grand under retail market. Wholesale is softer than it should be, which is why I called you.

What's your read on it?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Well, that's interesting about the mileage discrepancy and the wholesale being soft. But why are you offering so much above what a typical dealer might pay in wholesale? And how do I know this Carfax and AutoCheck info is legit and not just part of some elaborate scheme?

I'm not saying it isn't clean, but with a car like that, someone's gotta be giving up something. Usually it's either the condition, the paperwork, or both. So at thirty-one thousand five hundred, you're expecting me to pay a hefty markup even over retail? That doesn't add up for me without more reassurance on your end.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Fair question. Let me walk you through it straight.

First — the odometer thing. That's not hidden. It's flagged in both reports, and I led with it because it matters. Clean title, no frame damage, no salvage. That's verified data, not me selling you a story.

Second — why thirty-one five when MMR's at twenty-seven? Because wholesale markets move. MMR's a floor, not the ceiling. The rBook comps I'm looking at — actual dealer asking prices on similar mileage — they're forty grand plus. You're not paying retail. You're paying what the market's actually moving these at right now, which is somewhere between the wholesale floor and retail.

Here's what I'm NOT doing — I'm not asking you to take it on faith. You want to run your own Carfax? Pull the VIN. You want to send it to a third-party inspector before you commit? That's smart. Most of our partners do.

But here's the reality — if this car was beat up or the title was cloudy, I wouldn't have called you at all. The odometer variance is the headline because

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
It looks fishy and grabs attention. But the Carfax and AutoCheck reports are clean, and you're not trying to hide any issues with the car itself. That's good.

And you've got a point about MMR being just that - a minimum price for wholesale, not an asking price. If this car is truly trading at around $40k retail equivalent based on rBook comps despite only having ~60k miles, then your price isn't totally crazy.

I suppose what I'm saying is that the odometer reading discrepancy worries me, but you seem to have thought of that already and accounted for it in your pricing. If you're willing to give a bit there or make it easy for me to inspect before purchase, maybe we could find some common ground. 

But why reach out to just me? How do I know this isn't some sort of targeted sales pitch designed to get specific dealers to bid up the price higher than normal wholesale levels? I've been around the block enough times to know when something smells too good to be true.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
You're thinking like a sharp buyer — I respect that. Let me be direct.

I called you because you've been buying seven-fifties and your customer base runs loaded BMWs. That's not a conspiracy, that's just how this works. I match inventory to dealers who move it. You came to mind.

Now, on the odometer variance — I get why it's a sticking point. Here's what I can do: you inspect it before any money changes hands. Full pre-purchase inspection, your mechanic, however deep you want to go. If something doesn't check out, we walk back the number or walk away entirely. That's not a trick — it protects both of us.

On pricing — I'm not trying to bid you up against other dealers. This isn't an auction. I'm offering you a number I think works for your margin and your customer base. If it doesn't work, say so. But I'm not shopping this around trying to create artificial demand. That burns relationships.

Here's the truth: wholesale is soft right now on these. I'd rather move this car to somebody I trust at a solid number than chase retail or tie it up.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Okay Bill, you've given me some things to consider there. The pre-purchase inspection idea helps alleviate some of my concerns about the mileage discrepancy. And your explanation about targeting dealers who would actually buy and resell cars like that makes sense.

I still don't love how much above MMR you're asking for it - I've seen too many situations where someone tries to take advantage of a dealer's desperation or trust in them by pricing something way up there. But if you are truly just trying to match it with your customer base and not inflate prices artificially, then maybe that does make some sense.

I appreciate the honesty about wholesale being soft right now on these too - a lot of dealers I know have noticed that same trend. If you're willing to come down a bit from 31k5 to account for the mileage question and uncertainty around pricing, I might be able to stomach it enough to pull the trigger.

But I would still need some reassurance on your end about who your source is and why this particular car is priced so high compared to market conditions. Wholesale should generally be priced at or near MMR, not 30-40% above it without a very compelling reason. So before committing, I'd likely want you to

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
You're right to push back on the spread — that's fair. Let me give you the real picture here.

MMR at twenty-seven is what auction houses are moving similar units at. But that's auction volume pricing, not retail-equivalent market data. The rBook comps I'm seeing — actual dealer listings on sixty K mile seven-fifties — those are forty grand and up. We're targeting thirty-one five, which is nine grand under that retail ask. That's a healthy dealer margin, not price inflation.

Where the gap comes from: this car's loaded. Executive Package, Driving Assistance Pro, panoramic roof, massaging seats, ceramic controls. That option content adds real value over a stripper unit. MMR doesn't always price that out properly — it's a floor, not a precision tool.

On sourcing — it came through the normal channels. Clean title, verified miles discrepancy documented, no surprises.

Here's what I can do: I can come down to thirty grand if you commit to an inspection and move quick — say forty-eight hours. That gives you time to pull your mechanic through it, and me visibility that you're serious

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
I appreciate the further explanation there Bill. You've made some good points about MMR not always accounting for additional options or condition variations. And 40k retail comps on a 60k mile car with those packages is certainly pushing that price range.

Coming down to $30k flat if I commit quickly and get an inspection lined up sounds more reasonable. That's still above MMR, but in line with what the rBook comps seem to indicate a typical dealer asking price would be for something like this.

One last question - how much room do you have to negotiate that 31k5 number down? Because while $30k is closer to where I'm comfortable, I still think it could come down further and maintain a healthy margin for myself. If there's significant negotiating room on the price, that would make me more likely to pull the trigger.

I'd also like you to put in writing or at least confirm verbally that you won't be trying to shop this car around to other dealers while I'm evaluating it. You mentioned no auctions, but it never hurts to get it in writing.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Let me be straight with you on both fronts.

On price — thirty grand is my move. That's four hundred off MMR, which is solid for a unit with an odometer flag. Going lower and I'm eating into margin that doesn't make sense for us. I could shuffle terms — faster settlement, transport thrown in — but the number itself is where I land.

On the exclusivity piece — I hear you, but I can't lock it down in writing that I won't show it to other dealers. That's not how we operate. What I *can* tell you is this: you've got forty-eight hours from right now to get your inspector on it. You commit in that window, it's yours. After that, I move it. That's fair to both of us.

Here's what I'm doing though — while we're on the phone, can I text you the full bid card? That way you've got all the specs, the damage report, comps, everything in one place. You can sit with it, run it by your people, get your inspector lined up. Then you call me back and we lock it in.

Sound good?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Alright Bill, I think we have a deal then. $30k gets me looking at it for 48 hours, or the opportunity is moved on to someone else. And while we're on the phone, you can send over that full bid card with all the details so I can evaluate and get my inspector lined up.

One last thing - how quickly do you need me to respond after getting the bid card? Because I don't want this car to be listed again or shown to anyone else if I decide to pull the trigger. If I'm going to commit, I'd like it to be a quick decision so we can both move on and focus our efforts.

Otherwise, based on what you've told me and the details of that bid card, I'll make my best educated decision in that timeframe and let you know. Then we can seal the deal or agree to disagree and part ways. Sounds fair?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Sounds fair. Here's the timeline — you've got forty-eight hours from now. Get your inspector out there, pull the Carfax yourself if you want, run the numbers. When you're ready, call me back and we'll lock it in. Simple as that.

Let me send you that bid card right now.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Alright Bill, let's do this then. Send over that bid card so I can evaluate everything thoroughly in those 48 hours. If it checks out, I'll give you a call to commit and we'll seal the deal. Otherwise, no hard feelings, you move on.

Thank you for your time and patience walking through all of this with me. I appreciate a seller who is willing to discuss things openly and make concessions when there's legitimate concern or uncertainty on my end. That builds trust and makes deals get done.

I'll be in touch soon. Talk to you then.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Perfect. Bid card is on its way to you now — everything's in there. You've got forty-eight hours, Mike. Get your guy on it, do your due diligence. I'll be here.

Talk soon.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Hi Mike, this is Bill from Experience Wholesale. Do you have a moment?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: Bill from where now, the carnival? And you expect me to drop everything for this call? How do I know it's clean, Bill?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Okay, so here's the real story. Twenty-twenty-one 750i xDrive. Loaded — we're talking Executive Package, Driving Assistance Pro, panoramic roof, Nappa leather, the works. Carfax is clean. Title's clean.

Now, the heads-up: there's an odometer variance on the intake. We show fifty-nine eight at acquisition, Carfax shows fifty-five two. I want to lead with that because it matters to you — but the rest of the car is solid. No damage on either report.

MMR's at twenty-seven, rBook comps are running forty K. We're targeting thirty-one five. That puts us well below rBook and gives your customer real room on the retail side.

Could this work?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: Hmm, a BMW with all that trimmings, you say? Sounds almost too good to be true. Okay Bill, let's say I'm curious. How do I know this car hasn't been stolen and resold? There's nothing in Carfax or the title record that says it wasn't swiped off a dealer lot somewhere.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
That's a fair question. Clean title means it came through proper channels — no salvage, no lien, no branded history. The VIN matches the documentation. But you're right that a clean title doesn't tell you the whole story of where it lived before we got it.

Here's what I can tell you: we pulled this off auction. The auction house ran their intake on it — no flags there. And honestly, if there was theft history or any kind of fraud on the title, that would show up as a brand or a hold on the registration. A dealer lot theft would've surfaced as stolen recovered or rebuilt. None of that's there.

That said, if you want deeper intel on prior ownership or provenance before you commit, that's something an owner can dig into for you. Oscar can pull the full acquisition paperwork and walk you through exactly where this came from and who had it before we did.

Does that help, or is the odometer variance sitting with you weird enough that this one's a pass?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Well Bill, I must say you've managed to quell some of my concerns there. The clean title and auction background do provide some assurance it hasn't been stolen. Deeper intel on prior ownership would give me even more confidence.

But you know what else gives me pause? That big discrepancy in the odometer reading! Fifty-nine eight vs fifty-five two - that's a huge difference. I need to know why there's such a gap and if it's something fishy going on with the mileage. Could this just be an honest error, or is there more to it than meets the eye?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Good question, and I appreciate you digging into it. Look, the gap's real — four thousand five hundred miles is a spread worth understanding.

Here's what typically causes this: odometer discrepancies happen when a car's been serviced at multiple locations, or there's a delay between when we physically inspect it and when Carfax updates their records. Sometimes the first tech to pull the mileage gets it wrong, or the car's driven between the time we bid it and Carfax pulls their snapshot. It's not always nefarious — but it's also not nothing.

That said, I don't have the granular service history in front of me right now to tell you exactly which scenario this is. That's honestly the kind of detail Oscar needs to pull — he can get the full service records, cross-reference the dates Carfax scraped the mileage, and tell you whether this is a simple data lag or if there's something real to worry about.

What I will tell you: if there was actual fraud — rolled odometer, salvage history, whatever — that would be a title brand or a Carfax flag. We don't have

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
That makes more sense now Bill. A car with so much travel behind it is bound to have its mileage recorded by multiple parties. And I can see how those records may not sync up perfectly.

But even if there's no fraud, why would there be such a big gap? In my experience, that many miles missing from one reading could indicate something isn't right with the car itself. Is it possible this 750i has some extra wear and tear we should know about before we take a chance on it?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
That's solid thinking, and I hear where you're coming from. But here's the thing — the odometer variance doesn't necessarily mean there's hidden wear. Four grand is a gap, yeah, but it's not like the car's been driven hard in some fleet operation.

What it usually means is timing. Between when we took our reading at intake and when Carfax pulled their data, the car got driven. Or there's conflicting service records from different shops that haven't synced yet. Not wear and tear — just data lag.

The real indicator of condition is what we actually see: Carfax is clean, no structural damage, no flood, no accidents. The car runs. The options are all there and functional. If there was hidden mechanical wear from that extra mileage, that would show up as service records, collision history, or lemon flags. We don't have any of that.

Now, if you want a pre-purchase inspection to dig deeper — have your own tech look it over before you take delivery — that's completely fair and honestly smart practice on any unit. But based on what we're seeing in the records, this isn't a red flag for dur

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
I see, so it's just a matter of timing with those readings then. Well I suppose if Carfax is clean and there are no service records indicating wear or tear, that does give me some peace of mind.

But you're right Bill - a pre-purchase inspection would still be wise before I commit to this deal. I'll have one of my techs look it over once we've worked out the details. That should settle any lingering doubts about its condition and give us both confidence moving forward.

You know, as much as I appreciate your honesty and detail-oriented approach to this, I can't help but wonder how you ended up in wholesale after starting at Experience. It seems like a leap from working directly with customers to handling cars on the back end. What led you down this path?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Thanks, Mike — I appreciate that. Honestly, started on the lot, worked my way up to phones. You learn the business faster when you're touching the cars every day, and the phone side just felt like the natural next step.

Anyway, back to this seven-fifty. So you're feeling better about it — got your tech ready to do a PPI once we lock it in. That's smart, and honestly that's how these deals should work.

While we're on the phone, can I text you the bid card? Give you all the specs, the damage report, the comp data — everything your tech's gonna want to see before they climb under it.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
That sounds great Bill, sending over the bid card with all the details will certainly help me feel more prepared for this PPI.

Before we dive back into discussing this 750i, I have one last question - and you might find it strange coming from a dealer like myself. With car prices being what they are these days, would you say that this business of wholesale is sustainable long-term? The margins don't seem too high compared to how much dealers are paying for their inventory right now.

But then again, I suppose there's always money in the wholesale game - as long as there are dealers like me to buy your cars!

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Ha — yeah, there's always demand as long as we're sourcing right. Margins are tighter than they used to be, but that's true across the board. The game's about volume and turning inventory fast. We do that.

But look, I'm gonna send you that bid card right now so you've got everything in front of you.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Alright Bill, I'll wait for that bid card then. It sounds like Experience Wholesale has managed to adapt with the times - at least when it comes to the shifting landscape of the automotive industry.

I appreciate your willingness to provide me with detailed information and honestly answer my questions. That kind of transparency goes a long way in building trust with someone who's been burned before, like myself.

Once I've had my tech inspect the 750i, I'll give you a call back if it checks out. We can then discuss moving forward with this deal. Sounds fair?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Perfect. Sending that over now — you should see it in a few seconds. All the comps, the damage report, photos, everything your tech needs.

And yeah, sounds fair. Get your guy on it, and call me back once you've got eyes on it. We'll lock it in from there.

Appreciate you taking the time, Mike. Talk soon.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Hi Mike, this is Bill from Experience Wholesale. Do you have a moment?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: Bill from Experience Wholesale, huh? Well, Bill, I've gotta tell ya, I'm pretty skeptical about these calls outta the blue. But sure, I got a minute. What's on your mind?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Well, I appreciate you taking my call, Mike. I've got some high-quality product that just came in and I think it would be perfect for what you're looking for.

How do you know it's clean, Bill? And don't tell me "trust me," cuz we both know better than that by now.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Fair point. That's exactly why I led with the odometer flag — I'm not hiding it. Here's what we've got: Carfax is clean, no accidents, no structural damage, no flood. AutoCheck is clean too. The only disclosure is that mileage variance I mentioned — fifty-five K on the report, fifty-nine eight on the auction paperwork. We caught that on day one during intake and it's flagged in the title docs, so there's full transparency there.

The car itself runs solid. Loaded options — we're talking Executive Package, massaging seats, panoramic roof, the whole nine. At fifty-nine K miles on a twenty-twenty-one, it's in the sweet spot for your customer base. And the rBook comps are running thirty-nine to forty-three K depending on condition and miles.

So yeah — mechanically and title-wise, it's clean. The mileage variance is the one item, and it's all disclosed. What's your take on that?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Well, Bill, you're doing a better job answering my questions than most of these sales guys I deal with. But let's talk about this mileage discrepancy. 

You said it's only off by 4K, but when we're talking about cars... that's still a lot. Why so much above what the Motor Vehicle Report shows? And why hasn't it been aligned yet? That's not exactly a glowing endorsement of your attention to detail.

I'm curious too - what's the turnaround time on getting this mileage issue sorted out? I'd hate for a customer to get this car, then have you drag your feet on an alignment when they're like 'Hey, this odometer doesn't match what I was promised.' 

So, overall, it sounds alright, but that discrepancy worries me. I've been burned before with guys not being up front about these things. You seem honest so far, but I need more reassurance on the mileage situation before I'm willing to take a chance on this one. What's your plan B for sorting out that issue?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
You're asking the right questions, and I'm gonna level with you — that's exactly the kind of due diligence that keeps you out of trouble.

Here's the honest answer: the four-K gap is unusual. Could be the car rolled over between the Carfax report date and when we took it in, could be a data lag on the auction side. We flagged it immediately because that's the protocol — anything that doesn't line up gets disclosed, no exceptions. But I won't tell you we've got a magic fix for it yet.

What I can tell you is this: the title is clean. There's no branded status, no lien, nothing hiding. The disclosure is on paper. Your customer gets the car, they take it to their BMW dealer or an independent and have it inspected — if there's a real mechanical issue tied to that mileage discrepancy, it'll show up in service records or the engine condition. If it's just a data quirk, their tech will see that too.

Now, what I'm NOT gonna do is sit here and promise you we'll "align it" by some magic date. That's not how this works.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
I hear ya, Bill. You're right, there ain't no magic fixes when it comes to cars and numbers. 

But tell me this - if your customer takes the car in for an inspection and they find that mileage discrepancy doesn't match up with service records or engine condition, what's your recourse? Are you saying you'd replace the car outright or give a refund based on that?

Also, I'm wondering about warranties too. If I sell this car to one of my customers under your assurance it's clean, and something goes wrong down the line related to that mileage discrepancy... do we have any protection in place for that? Because if not, that's gonna be a big red flag for me.

You've got me intrigued with this deal, no doubt. But I need some solid reassurance on those two points before I'm ready to take the leap. I can't risk my reputation selling cars that might fall apart later because of a shady odometer game. So what's your final word on these issues?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
I'm gonna be straight with you, Mike — those are owner-level questions, and I don't want to promise you something I can't personally back. Warranty terms, post-sale adjustments, refund guarantees — that's Oscar's lane, not mine. I'd rather get him in front of you to lock that in properly than have me say "yeah, we'll take it back" and then there's confusion later.

What I can tell you is this: we stand behind our inventory. Clean title, full disclosure on the odo flag — that's what you're buying. But the specifics on coverage and recourse? That needs to come from the principal.

Here's what I'm thinking — you sound like you're genuinely interested in the car, you're just being smart about the terms. So let me send you the full bid card right now while we're on the phone, and I'll have Oscar ring you back today to walk through the warranty and protection side. He can answer those questions way better than I can, and you'll have it in writing.

Can I text you the information?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Well, Bill, that sounds fair enough. If Oscar can give me a bit more clarity on the warranty and refund policies, I'm okay with waiting for his call to go ahead and take another look at this car.

One last question before we wrap up here - do you have any history with these cars? I know Experience Wholesale handles all kinds of inventory, but have you had good luck with BMWs in particular lately?

I want to make sure my customers are getting a vehicle they can count on. I'd hate to send them down the road in something that's going to leave 'em stranded or worse. So what's your track record been like with these Beemers?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Good question. Honestly, the seven-fifty market's been soft across the board — we're seeing that reflected in the spread here. But the units we move tend to be solid when they're prepped right. This one in particular, aside from that mileage flag, checks out mechanically. The options load and condition support the rBook comps we're seeing.

That said — I'm not gonna sit here and spin you a story about our BMW track record when what matters is THIS car in front of you right now. You're doing your homework, and that's what keeps your customers happy. Get it inspected, verify the mechanical condition yourself, and let Oscar give you the warranty details. Then you'll know exactly what you're working with.

Alright, let me send you that bid card now. Give me a second.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
You've got a deal, Bill. I appreciate your honesty - it's hard to find people these days who are straightforward about these things. 

I'll take a look at the bid card and wait for Oscar to touch base with some more details on the warranty stuff. Like you said, my customers come first, so I need to make sure they're getting something reliable before I put them in play.

Thanks for taking the time to walk me through this. I'm gonna hold off making a decision until I get all my ducks in a row, but this is definitely food for thought. 

I'll chat with you soon, Bill. Take care out there.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Appreciate that, Mike. You're doing it right — your customers are lucky to have someone who vets things that carefully. Watch for that text, and Oscar will be in touch today to lock down the warranty piece. Talk soon.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Hi Mike, this is Bill from Experience Wholesale. Do you have a moment?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: *pauses and looks suspicious* What's the pitch, Bill? I'm not interested in any of that nonsense again. You'd be wasting your time.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Hey, I hear you. Look, I'm not gonna waste your time — I just caught something that actually fits what you've been buying. Twenty-one BMW seven-fifty-i, sixty thousand miles, loaded. Got an odometer flag I'm leading with because that's the real talk. Otherwise clean. We're at thirty-one five. Could this work?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
*raises eyebrow skeptically* Thirty-one and a half thousand for that car, huh? *takes a drag on mental cigarette* And you expect me to believe it hasn't been touched in sixty grand? 

How do you know the title's clean? I've seen shady deals before where some "friend of a friend" says they got an honest salvage job. Then the plates go on another vehicle and bingo, more parts to sell. Can't be that.

And your source for this thing - he got it from someone reliable or is it just some guy who found it in his backyard? 

Lastly, why are you asking so much above KBB (Kelly Blue Book) value? Lot of nice options but even loaded, thirty-one and a half thousand's a tall glass. Something else going on here?

You're gonna have to prove to me this isn't another scam. Can't just take your word for it anymore than I can eat your word for being a delicious meal. You understand that, Bill?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
I get it — you've been burned before. Let me be straight with you.

First, the title's clean. Not salvage, not branded. We pull Carfax and AutoCheck on everything before it hits the lot. That odometer variance I mentioned — it's in there. Fifty-five thousand two hundred eighty-three on Carfax, fifty-nine eight on the odometer itself. We flagged it on intake, that's why I led with it.

Source is auction. Not backyard, not some guy — legitimate lane. We know where it came from.

On the price — you're right to push back. Here's the real math: rBook's sitting at forty-two. We're at thirty-one five, which is four and a half grand ABOVE MMR but eight grand UNDER rBook. That spread's where the value is for you. You're not paying retail, and you're not catching a unit with hidden problems. It's a fair ask.

But here's the thing — I'm not asking you to take my word. I'm gonna send you the full bid card right now. Carfax, photos, all

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
*leans back in chair* Alright Bill, I'll give you this - you're giving me more than just your "word". Photos and a Carfax report, that's good. But let me look it over first before I say yes.

And about MMR - my boy Joe told me once 'never pay MMR for anything'. Best way to get stuck with an overpriced lemon. But you're right it ain't retail, so there's some wiggle room. 

You've got my curiosity piqued though not full approval yet. I'll need a few days to check it out and think on it. You understand that?

I don't trust unsolicited calls from shady characters trying to offload questionable vehicles at inflated prices. But you're giving me things to look at, proof of where it came from. Not asking for the moon either.

I've been around long enough to know there's no such thing as a free lunch or a dirt cheap car that's too good to be true isn't. Gotta poke holes in these deals, Bill. I'm glad you're letting me do my job instead of trying to rush me into something.

If the Carfax checks out and those photos match what's actually there

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
I appreciate that, Mike. You're doing exactly what you should be doing — poking holes, verifying before you move. That's how you stay sharp in this business.

Let me send you the bid card right now while we're talking. That way you've got everything in front of you — photos, Carfax, the full rundown on options, where we're priced. You can sit with it for a few days, kick the tires on the numbers, and if it still makes sense, we'll lock it in.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
*chuckles* Kick the tires on the numbers, huh? I like that. Alright Bill, send me this bid card over. Let me chew on it a bit and see what my gut tells me.

You've got something here, it's not total BS at least. But you know as well as I do - in this business it's always good to trust but verify. 

I'll look it all over, check your numbers against my sources' values, make sure the Carfax matches up with what we're seeing in the photos. If everything checks out after a few days of looking at it, maybe we got ourselves a deal.

But if not - and believe me Bill, things have fallen apart on me before when I didn't do my homework - then you'll be hearing from a very unhappy old man who just wasted time he doesn't got to spare. 

So make sure what you're sending is the real deal this time. I don't need another dead end after chasing down false leads. 

But if it's legit, we might be able to do something here. Get me that bid card and let's see where things go from there.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Perfect. It's heading your way right now, Mike. You've got the full bid card — all the photos, Carfax, the breakdown on every option. Everything's legit. No surprises.

Take your time. Kick those numbers around with your sources, compare what you're seeing. You do your homework the right way, and if it all lines up, we'll make it happen.

Talk soon.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Hi Mike, this is Bill from Experience Wholesale. Do you have a moment?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: Bill, I'm always skeptical of unsolicited calls claiming to be from wholesale providers. How do I know it's really you and not some scammer trying to trick me into buying dirty product?

transcript.text:
Alright Bill, I'll bite. What kind of product are we talking about here? And how do you know it's clean and not cut or laced with anything dangerous? That's a major concern for me.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
I think there's a misunderstanding here — we're talking about used cars, not anything else. Experience Wholesale is a wholesale auto dealer. We buy and sell vehicles at auction.

On the BMW I'm calling about: it's a twenty-twenty-one seven-fifty-i, fifty-nine thousand eight hundred miles, loaded with the Executive Package and Driving Assistance Pro. Black Sapphire exterior, black interior. Clean Carfax except for one flag I want to lead with — odometer variance. The Carfax shows fifty-five thousand two hundred eighty-three, but we clocked it at fifty-nine eight on intake. I'm transparent about that because it matters to your deal.

As for price: MMR's sitting at twenty-seven grand. We're asking thirty-one five. That spread is typical for a loaded seven-fifty with that low mileage and condition. RBook's at forty-two, so you're still nine grand under book. Could this work for your lot?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Whoa, okay - I'm coming back to Earth now. A car dealer calling me! Well, it's certainly more believable than a "wholesale" pitch for something else.

Alright Bill, let me get this straight: You've got a 2021 BMW 750i with the Executive Package and Driving Assistance Pro. 59k miles, priced at $31,500, versus an MMR of $27,000. 

The Carfax shows 55k2 on the odometer but you've checked it yourself and say it's actually 59000. That odometer variance is definitely something to be transparent about. 

And I see what you're saying about the price - $31,500 is still a solid number compared to what Book says cars like this are going for.

So my question then becomes: What kind of shape is this vehicle in? Are there any other issues or quirks we should know about aside from that odometer reading? And how can you guarantee these cars won't have any title troubles down the line? That's a big concern when dealing with used vehicles.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Okay, got the full card here. On condition: both Carfax and AutoCheck are clean — no accidents, no flood, no frame damage, nothing structural. The only flag is that odometer variance I mentioned upfront.

Title's clean and clear. No liens, no brand, no salvage history. Straight resale title.

As for the condition otherwise: this is a loaded machine. Panoramic Sky Lounge roof, ventilated and massaging front seats, Nappa leather, dark oak trim, Harmon Kardon sound, wireless charging, gesture control, WiFi hotspot — the list goes on. It's a forty-one thousand dollar option list on a one-hundred-eleven grand MSRP.

The wholesale market on sevens is soft right now — you'll see retail asking forty, forty-two grand, but dealer auction prices are in the twenty-six to twenty-eight range. That's why thirty-one five works: you've got room to land it in your lot and move it. Interested in taking a closer look?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Alright Bill, you've piqued my interest with that loaded feature list! It sounds like this BMW is in great shape overall aside from the honest odometer read we discussed.

At $31,500 it seems like fair value based on what we're seeing with MMR and Book prices. The fact it's got a clear title is also reassuring for someone like me looking to flip these vehicles without headaches down the line.

I'm curious though - how did this vehicle come into your inventory? Was it an auction buy or acquired some other way? And have you had good luck flipping similar high-end used cars at prices like $31,500?

Those are my main concerns. If we can iron those details out I might be interested in taking a closer look and maybe even placing a bid if the numbers add up for my lot. What do you say, Bill?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Good questions. On sourcing: this one came through auction — Manheim, standard wholesale channel. Clean title going in, clean coming out.

On flipping sevens at this price point: honestly, the market's soft right now like I said. Retail's asking forty to forty-two, but velocity's slower than it was two years ago. That said, at thirty-one five you're positioned to move it. RBook comps I'm looking at show dealers landing similar mileage and condition in the thirty-five to forty range, so you've got a two to four grand spread if your customer base is there.

The real question is whether your lot moves seven-fifty-i's and whether your buyer wants loaded luxury or if they're looking for something leaner. You know your floor better than I do.

That said — you're interested enough to look closer, yeah? While we're on the phone, can I text you the full bid card? You can see the photos, the Carfax detail, all the options laid out. You'll have everything in one spot.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
You've given me a lot to think about, Bill. The numbers make sense and the vehicle checks out well on paper from what you're telling me.

I'm leaning towards taking another look at this BMW 750i of yours but I need to make sure it's worth my time and investment in shipping it up here before placing a bid. If you can send over that full bid card with details, Carfax printout, and photos like you mentioned, I'll be able to get a better visual of the vehicle.

I appreciate your patience explaining things thoroughly too - that's important when dealing with someone who's been burned before! 

So let's say I take a closer look at this one. Are there any other high-end used vehicles like this in your inventory or coming up through auction you'd recommend keeping an eye out for? I'm always on the hunt for quality product to move quickly.

Bill, keep me posted and don't hesitate to reach back out if anything changes with this BMW or if more suitable vehicles come across your desk. My curiosity is definitely piqued now!

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Perfect — you should see that text come through in a few seconds. Full bid card, photos, Carfax summary, the whole package.

On other inventory: I'm gonna be straight with you — I don't want to throw out names on cars I haven't dug into the same way. What I will do is get a read on what's coming through this week and next. You looking for sevens specifically, or are you open to other luxury sedans — five series, S-Class, that kind of lane? And what's your sweet spot on mileage and price range? I'll keep my eyes open and call you when something lands that actually fits.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
I appreciate the honesty there Bill - it's good to know exactly where everyone stands.

In terms of inventory, since we're on the topic: I'm keeping an eye out for loaded luxury sedans in the $25-35k range with 50-80k miles max. I'll snag up a few quality BMWs and Mercedes that fit those numbers if they come across your radar. 

The sweet spot seems to be around $30k mark, especially if we can get something like this 750i at $31,500. Those prices move for me without too much trouble in my market.

You keep an eye out and let me know when you find some potential candidates that fit within those parameters. I'm always looking to add a few good ones to the lot to flip over! If anything aligns with what we discussed, I'll certainly consider taking another look.

Sounds like a solid plan to make this work Bill. Keep me posted on any promising finds and we'll see where this goes.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Sounds good, Mike. I've got your profile locked in — twenty-five to thirty-five K, fifty to eighty K miles, loaded luxury sedans. BMW and Mercedes your main lanes. I'll flag anything that hits those marks and ring you back.

One of the owners will follow up with you on this seven-fifty to lock it in if you decide to move on it after you look it over. Don't hesitate to call me back if you've got questions on the bid card or want to negotiate the number.

Talk soon, Mike.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Hi Mike, this is Bill from Experience Wholesale. Do you have a moment?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: Bill from where now, the phone book? And what makes you think I got a moment to spare for you?

transcript.text: Ha, fair enough. Experience Wholesale down in Mount Dora — and I get it, you're busy. Just need a minute though. Got a twenty-twenty-one BMW seven-fifty that came in, sixty K miles, loaded. Thought you'd want the first look before it hits the floor.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: Okay Bill, let's say I'm interested, which I'm not sure about yet. What makes you think this car is clean? And who exactly are these people we're buying from? I need sources, Bill. You understand.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Alright, here's the story. Both Carfax and AutoCheck are clean — no accidents, no title issues, nothing structural. That's the baseline. But I'm gonna lead with the headline: there's an odometer variance on this one. The bid came in at fifty-nine eight, but Carfax shows fifty-five two eighty-three. That's a four-thousand-five-hundred-mile difference. I flagged it on intake, so you know it going in.

Beyond that, this thing is loaded. Driving Assistance Pro, Executive Package, panoramic roof, heated and massage seats, nice dark oak trim. Black Sapphire paint. Sixty K on the clock, runs solid.

On price — rBook's sitting at forty K-range on these, but the wholesale market is soft. MMR's at twenty-seven. We're at thirty-one five. That puts you nine grand under rBook and in the sweet spot for your buyer base. You still moving stuff in that segment?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: You say variance in the odometer, but how do I know it's not a wash job or some other kind of shady work done to the car? And why this much above market value, Bill? What am I missing here?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Good questions — both legit. On the odo variance first: we pulled this off auction, not a private buy. The mile discrepancy hit our intake flag the second we ran Carfax. Could be a data lag between when the car was appraised and when it actually rolled through the auction block — happens. Could be the previous owner had a cluster issue that got fixed. Point is, it's documented and disclosed. You're not flying blind. If you want to dig deeper, you can run your own Carfax or AutoCheck before you commit.

On the thirty-one five being above market — you're reading it right, but here's the nuance. MMR at twenty-seven is the basement wholesale number. That's fire-sale territory. RBook at forty is what retail dealers are asking. We're right in the middle where your customer actually lives. Look at the rBook comps I pulled — you've got similar mileage Sapphire blacks around thirty-five to forty depending on condition. We're nine grand under that. 

The spread exists because these seven-fifty is are depreciating like rocks right now — luxury sedan market is soft.

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
That variance still doesn't sit well with me, but alright, I see your point about it being disclosed and not fly-by-night. As for the pricing, you may have something there, given where things stand in that segment. 

What's your commission on this deal looking like? And how long can you guarantee these numbers if I decide to pull the trigger today?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
I appreciate you asking straight up. Honestly, commission structure isn't something I handle — that's Oscar and the ownership side. What I can tell you is our target's locked in at thirty-one five, and that's good through today. If you want to move on it, I'll get you the bid card right now via text with all the specs, condition report, the odo flag, everything. Then one of the owners will lock in terms with you — payment window, any floor plan, the whole deal.

What's your timeline looking like? Are we talking you want to move fast, or you need to sit with it a day?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
I might need a day or two to look over all the paperwork and maybe run my own Carfax check. This isn't an impulse buy for me, Bill. 

As for a timeline, I've been looking at some nice cars lately but nothing concrete yet. If this one pans out and checks out, I'd love to get it by Friday or sooner. But as I said, give me till Monday just in case.

How do you feel about that, Bill?

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
That's smart — I'd do the same thing. You run your own Carfax, kick the tires however you need to. No pressure.

Here's what I'm gonna do: I'll text you the full bid card right now so you've got everything in front of you. You can digest it over the next couple days, run your checks, and if it still makes sense, reach out. We can lock it in and get you the car by Friday easy.

Can I send that to you now?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Sure Bill, send away. If what you've said holds up and I'm satisfied with the details when they come, we might have something here.

But remember, don't take anything for granted with me. I'll need hard facts and figures, not just a story. Check your messaging — it better be on point or else.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
You got it. Text is on the way now — full bid card with the damage audit, the specs breakdown, the rBook comps, everything. No fluff, just facts. Odo variance is right there in black and white so you can see exactly what we're working with.

You run your own Carfax, dig into it however deep you need. If the numbers hold and you're good to go, just ring me back or text and we'll get one of the owners to close it out. Friday delivery is doable if you move by Wednesday.

Sound fair?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
I guess it sounds fair enough, Bill. I appreciate the detail in your message this time around. 

Like I said, I'll take a look at everything when it comes through and go from there. If it's all legitimate and checks out, we might have ourselves a deal.

But don't get too excited yet. I've been scammed before and I'm not about to let it happen again. So if I find anything amiss or off in your facts, you better believe I'll be on you like white on rice.

Good luck, Bill. We'll see where this goes from here.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
I hear you loud and clear, Mike. That's the right attitude — verify everything. I'd expect nothing less. You find anything that doesn't line up, you call me back and we sort it out straight. No games on our end.

Talk soon.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text: Hi Mike, this is Bill from Experience Wholesale. Do you have a moment?

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text: Bill, I'm always cautious when it comes to these types of deals. How do I know this product is clean? And who exactly is your source? These are things that are important to me.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Okay, Mike — I'm gonna be straight with you because that's how we work. It's a twenty-twenty-one BMW seven-fifty-i, fifty-nine eight hundred miles, loaded. Here's what matters to you: Carfax and AutoCheck both come back clean. No accidents, no structural damage, nothing hidden.

Now — and I'm leading with this because you asked about clean — there IS one flag: odometer mismatch between what we bid at auction and what Carfax shows. Fifty-nine eight hundred on our side, fifty-five two eighty-three on Carfax. That's a four-thousand-five-hundred gap. I'm flagging it because it's material, and you deserve to know it upfront. Could be a data lag from the service records, could be other reasons — but it's there.

As for sourcing: we caught this off a recent auction transaction. Not a lease return, not a trade-in flip — straight auction acquisition. The wholesale market on these has been soft, which is actually why I'm calling you. The retail comps are running thirty-nine to forty-two on similar

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Bill, I appreciate your transparency and the detailed explanation. However, there's still something bothering me about this whole situation. 

The odometer discrepancy sounds a bit suspicious. I've been around the block, and that seems like a red flag to me. What makes you so sure it's not tampered with or something? Also, why would the retail market be soft for these vehicles right now?

I'm not saying no - just needing more clarity before jumping in. It's my nature after previous experiences.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Mike, I hear you — that's fair. You've been burned before, and I'm not gonna blow smoke at you.

On the odometer thing: yeah, it's a legitimate question. Here's what I know for certain — both Carfax AND AutoCheck flagged the variance, which means it's documented and reported. If there was actual tampering, those systems would catch structural inconsistencies in the service records and mileage timeline. What we're looking at here is a DATA discrepancy, not evidence of fraud. Could be the auction house input it one way, Carfax pulled it another. Could be a service record timing thing. But the fact that it's TRANSPARENT on both reports — not hidden — that's actually a good sign. A dealer trying to hide something wouldn't let both bureaus see the gap.

Now, the soft market on seven-fifty-i's — that's real and it's in your favor. Look, the new ones are still running over one-ten on MSRP. But the used market? Retail's asking thirty-nine to forty-two. Wholesale — what we're seeing at auction — is running much lower

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Bill, I understand what you're saying about the data discrepancy, and it does sound more convincing now. However, there's still that small voice in my head telling me not to rush into this without being 100% sure.

Let me ask you this - do you have any contacts within the auction house or Carfax/AutoCheck themselves? I'd feel a lot better if we could get some direct confirmation about the odometer issue before moving forward. 

Also, have you been able to research why the retail market is soft for these vehicles right now? That info would definitely help me make a more informed decision.

Again, I'm not saying no - just trying to gather all the facts before proceeding. It's been a long road teaching me this lesson.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Mike, I respect that caution — truly. But I'm gonna be honest with you: I don't have direct contacts inside Carfax or the auction house that I can call and pull individual case files on. That's not how the system works, and if I told you I could do that, I'd be blowing smoke.

What I CAN tell you is this — both bureaus have already documented the variance. It's in their systems. If there was fraud, THEY would flag it as suspected odometer rollback. They haven't. The data's just inconsistent. That's the confirmation you're looking at right now.

On the soft market — that's broader industry stuff. These seven-fifties are getting older, there's new models coming, and honestly, the V8 luxury sedan market just isn't what it was three years ago. Dealers are holding them longer, asking prices are sticky, but wholesale values keep sliding. That's why this unit at our number — thirty-one five — is actually a solid play for you. You're buying below where the market's trending.

Here's my read, Mike: you're looking for a guarantee I can't give you

transcript.speaker: PARTNER
transcript.text:
Bill, I hear you, and I appreciate your honesty. You've provided some good insights, but there's still that lingering doubt in my mind. It's hard to shake after so many years of dealing with people who aren't always on the up-and-up.

You mentioned the inconsistency being a confirmation of sorts - but it's not exactly what I was hoping for. I want direct verification from an authorized source before making any decisions. 

As for the market situation, your explanation makes sense and does give me something to consider. The price point is definitely attractive given the current trends you've outlined.

I'm not saying no, Bill - but I do need more time to think about this. It's a big decision and I want to be sure it's the right one for my business. Let's touch base in a couple days and maybe by then I'll have some more questions or concerns that we can address together. 

I appreciate your patience and understanding on this matter.

transcript.speaker: BILL
transcript.text:
Mike, I get it. You've built your business on being careful, and that's smart — that's kept you in the game this long.

Here's what I'm gonna do: I want to send you the full bid card on this unit right now. You'll have all the photos, the damage audit, the Carfax report, the rBook comps — everything. You can sit with it, dig into it on your own time, run it past whoever you trust. No pressure.

transcript.text: Hi Mike, this is Bill from Experience Wholesale. Do you have a moment?

transcript.text:
